title: Gathering Clouds: Building Digital Strategic Depth in the Compute Age
gist: A Microsoft policy lead argues that in a data-centric age strategic depth must be measured in the resilience of data and compute, not geography — and that governments' instinct to build sovereign owned data centers creates brittle targets, favoring distributed hyperscale cloud with dispersion, elasticity, and optionality.

themes.theme: Digital strategic depth as new center of gravity

insights.claim: In a data-centric age, digital infrastructure has become part of the battlespace, and as physical distance loses its protective value, strategic depth must be measured in the resilience of data and compute rather than geography.
insights.speaker: Blake Herzinger
insights.so_what: Reframes data-center resilience as a national-security requirement, expanding the addressable market for hyperscale cloud.
insights.locator: The wars in Ukraine and the Middle East

insights.claim: Data centers and cloud regions are the digital backbone of military and economic power and now present attractive targets for proliferating long-range strike systems, drones, and cyber capabilities.
insights.speaker: Blake Herzinger
insights.so_what: Positions data infrastructure as a top-tier physical and cyber target set requiring new resilience spend.
insights.locator: Data centers and cloud regions are now

insights.claim: Digital strategic depth rests on three pillars — dispersion (distribution to complicate targeting), elasticity (shifting workloads to absorb shock), and optionality (avoiding single points of failure including vendor lock-in).
insights.speaker: Blake Herzinger
insights.so_what: A usable framework for evaluating which cloud and infrastructure architectures governments will buy.
insights.locator: It is achieved through three mutually reinforcing pillars

insights.claim: Governments risk mistaking local control for real resilience — a single fixed sovereign data center, however well defended, remains a fixed target, and if a network has an address an adversary can plan against it.
insights.speaker: Blake Herzinger
insights.so_what: Challenges the data-localization policy wave, favoring distributed over sovereign-owned infrastructure.
insights.locator: The instinct to build and own sovereign data centers

insights.claim: Sovereignty and data survivability trade off on a sliding scale: ratcheting up localization decreases survivability and increases costs, so governments are not best served by maximizing any single input.
insights.speaker: Blake Herzinger
insights.so_what: Gives a policy argument for hybrid architectures over pure localization mandates.
insights.locator: This argument does not deny the tension

insights.claim: Cryptographic controls, sovereign access models, and hybrid architectures now let states retain sovereign control over data without controlling it locally, dissolving the old binary between domestic storage and surrendering control.
insights.speaker: Blake Herzinger
insights.so_what: Technical enablers that unlock hyperscale-cloud adoption by sovereignty-conscious governments.
insights.locator: However, governments no longer face a binary choice

insights.claim: Ukraine enhanced resilience by storing data and critical warfighting applications on cloud infrastructure outside the country, placing operational advantages beyond Russian kinetic reach while its DELTA platform fuses tens of terabytes of data daily.
insights.speaker: Blake Herzinger
insights.so_what: A live proof that offshore cloud creates strategic depth through data architecture, not geography.

insights.claim: Iran's strikes on Middle East data centers caused outages because multiple availability zones went offline simultaneously while the redundancy model only accounted for losing one zone, though providers mitigated by migrating workloads out of the target area.
insights.speaker: Blake Herzinger
insights.so_what: Concentrating data centers in a regional cluster serving that same region is the specific vulnerability to avoid.
insights.locator: In Iran’s strikes on data centers

insights.claim: Fires at South Korean data centers in 2022 and 2025 disrupted hundreds of government services, showing concentration creates strategic vulnerability even in peacetime.
insights.speaker: Blake Herzinger
insights.so_what: Even routine accidents make the case for distributed over centralized digital infrastructure.
insights.locator: Even routine accidents reveal the fragility

insights.claim: Over a decade ago the US Air Force already collected roughly six petabytes of data per day, and systems like Palantir's Maven Smart System and Ukraine's DELTA now show that aggregated, processed data yields real-time operational advantage.
insights.speaker: Blake Herzinger
insights.so_what: Rising military data volumes and AI integration drive escalating demand for scalable resilient compute.
insights.locator: At the same time, military power has become

insights.claim: The Joint Warfighting Cloud Capability Unified Cloud Marketplace makes survivability its first objective and mandates data centers at least 400 miles apart on separate power grids, points of presence on all continents but Antarctica, and vendor on-ramp/off-ramp to avoid lock-in.
insights.speaker: Blake Herzinger
insights.so_what: A concrete US procurement template signaling multicloud, geographically-dispersed demand for cloud vendors.
insights.locator: A solicitation for the future of the Joint Warfighting Cloud

insights.claim: The author, Microsoft's defense and national security policy lead for Asia, discloses a direct commercial interest in hyperscale-cloud adoption but argues the strategic benefit is not exclusive to his employer.
insights.speaker: Blake Herzinger
insights.so_what: Weigh the piece as an interested-party case for hyperscale cloud over sovereign data centers.

insights.claim: The author warns that merely moving on-premises operating models into the cloud would reproduce the Maginot Line in digital form — a fixed architecture built around control when the requirement is survivability through dispersion, elasticity, and optionality.
insights.speaker: Blake Herzinger
insights.so_what: Lift-and-shift cloud migration misses the strategic point; architecture, not location, is the differentiator.
insights.locator: Merely moving existing on-premises operating models
